turns.text: Hello Dian, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It feels a bit strange to talk about this out loud, to be honest. I’m ten weeks in, walking around Fall River trying to act like my usual self, but my whole world has tilted. I’m twenty-seven, I’m on my own, and sometimes the silence in my apartment at night is just… loud. It makes every little twinge or cramp feel like a potential disaster, even though my midwife keeps telling me I’m “low-risk” and doing just fine.

I think I’m trying to overcompensate by being the perfect patient. I spent half the night researching Tdap boosters and flu shots because I’m terrified of missing something I’m supposed to know. I want to be ahead of the curve, but I worry that because my charts look so boring—no complications, no issues—the medical team sees me as just another file on a shelf. They don’t see the panic I feel when I think about coming home to an empty room after the baby is born. 

Seeing that tiny flicker on the monitor, though… that changed everything. For a minute, I wasn't just a clinical data point. I was actually a mother. It was beautiful, but it also made the stakes feel so much higher. I just need someone to acknowledge that even if the medicine is easy, the journey is really heavy.
When you think about your goals for starting or growing your family, what were the most important outcomes you hoped to achieve — for yourself, your relationship, and your future child?

Honestly, I’m not sure I even had a "plan" that looks like what you see in the brochures. I didn't get to check the boxes of marriage or having a partner to share this with, so my goals had to shift really quickly. For me, the biggest thing is stability—not just financial, though that’s a massive stressor right now—but emotional stability. I want to be the kind of mother who is steady and grounded, even when the world feels like it’s spinning.

My goal for this child is simple: I want them to know they were wanted, completely and entirely, right from the start. I’m working so hard to make sure I’m informed and ready because I don’t have a second set of hands at home to help me figure things out. I’m terrified of the unknown, but I’m determined to do this right. 

As for me, I just want to come out of this feeling like I didn't lose myself. I’m scared that between the medical appointments and the solitary nights, I’ll just disappear into being a "patient" or just a "mom." I want to keep some of who I am, even while I’m building this whole new life from scratch. It’s a lot to carry on your own, you know?

When you think about your motivations for seeking preconception care or health advice before becoming pregnant, what was it that prompted you to take action — or, if you did not seek any care, what held you back?

To be completely honest, things didn't happen in the "ideal" order. It wasn't like I had a scheduled sit-down with a doctor before I saw that positive test. When the realization hit me, it felt like I’d been thrown into the deep end of the ocean. My first instinct—my only instinct, really—was to protect this little life, which is exactly why I became such a fanatic about appointments the second I knew.

I think I’m acting this way because I don't have that cushion of a partner to debate decisions with or double-check things. If I don't get the information from my midwife or from reliable sources, who else is going to catch it? That’s what pushed me to start asking about the vaccination schedule so early. I’m just trying to build my own safety net out of facts and medical guidelines because I don't have the luxury of "winging it." 

I felt like if I could just follow the clinical rules perfectly, I could prove that I’m capable of doing this, even without a partner. It’s my way of exerting control when everything else feels so overwhelming and uncertain. I just want to make sure I’m giving my baby the best, healthiest start possible, even if I have to piece it together on my own.
What specifically triggered that decision at that moment rather than earlier or later?

It was the second that test turned positive, the room just felt like it went dead silent. That was the moment everything stopped being hypothetical. Before that, my life was just me—going to work, walking down Main Street, worrying about rent—but then I was looking at that little stick and realized I was suddenly responsible for someone who didn't even have a voice yet. 

It wasn't a choice to "seek care," it was a primal need to make sure I wasn't doing something wrong. I had this immediate, crushing fear that because I was doing this alone, I had zero room for error. I couldn't afford to be naive or uninformed. I think I felt a desperate need to be validated by the medical system, like if a doctor told me I was doing fine, it would somehow make the fact that I’m single and doing this solo okay. 

I’m not the type to sit back and wait. Once I knew, I felt like every single day I didn't have a plan was a day I was failing. So, I started reading. I started calling clinics. I just needed to hear a professional tell me that I was on the right track, because deep down, I’m still just terrified that I’m going to drop the ball.

When you think about how you actually behaved during the preconception period — your lifestyle choices, health actions, information-seeking — what did you do, and how did those behaviours reflect what you truly believed was important?

Looking back, I realize I was trying to "parent" myself before the baby even arrived. My behavior was all about control. Before I knew I was pregnant, I was probably living like any other twenty-seven-year-old—rushing through my days, grabbing coffee on the run, not really thinking about my body as a vessel. But the second that test came back positive, I turned into a drill sergeant. 

I stopped everything that felt "risky." I became hyper-vigilant about what I ate, what I touched, even the cleaning supplies I used in my apartment. I think this reflects my core belief that because I don’t have a partner to share the load, the burden of "getting it right" is entirely on my shoulders. I felt like if I slipped up, it would be my fault—not just a stroke of bad luck. 

My information-seeking became a coping mechanism. I’d spend hours on reputable medical sites, terrified of forums with too many opinions, just looking for concrete, clinical, non-negotiable rules. I truly believe that knowledge is the only armor I have. By researching vaccines and prenatal nutrition, I wasn't just checking boxes; I was trying to build a foundation that felt solid enough to hold a human being. It was my way of saying, "I am taking this seriously, and I am enough."

When you think about your emotional experience during the preconception period — the waiting, the uncertainty, and the anticipation — what feelings stand out most strongly, and were any of those feelings hard to acknowledge or talk about?

The biggest feeling, the one I couldn't really voice to anyone, was this heavy sense of *isolation*. People talk about pregnancy like it’s this joyful, communal experience—babyshowers, partners rubbing your back, family excitement—but for me, the waiting felt like being under a magnifying glass all by myself. 

There was a lot of shame hidden in there, too. I’m twenty-seven, and I think I had this internal script that said, "Dian, you should have it all figured out by now." Admitting that I was scared to be a single mother felt like admitting I was failing before I’d even started. I couldn't tell my friends, "Hey, I’m terrified that I’m going to be a bad mom because there’s no one else here to help me check my work." It felt safer to just focus on the medical facts, like the vaccination schedules and the ultrasound dates, because those are objective. You can’t argue with a heartbeat on a screen, but you *can* argue with the feeling that you’re not ready.

I think I was mostly mourning the life I thought I was supposed to have, while simultaneously trying to be grateful for the life I actually do have. Balancing those two things—the grief and the hope—was the hardest part, and it’s still really hard to say out loud.

When you think about the barriers you encountered — or anticipated — in your path to conception or early pregnancy preparation, what were the most significant obstacles, and how did you navigate them?

The biggest barrier has been the wall between being a "patient" and being a "person." When I walk into that clinic in Fall River, I feel like I’m in a high-speed lane—vitals, weight, blood pressure, "everything looks good, see you in four weeks." That’s all fine and well for the baby's health, but nobody ever asks, "Dian, how are you handling the quiet at home? Who is your support system when the middle-of-the-night panic hits?"

The obstacle isn't that I don't have access to care; it’s that the care feels transactional. I’ve had to navigate this by essentially teaching myself how to advocate for my own mental space. I started writing down my questions—not just the medical ones about Tdap or vitamins, but the ones about postpartum and how to manage the exhaustion solo. I force myself to ask them, even when I feel like I’m being "that" patient who talks too much or takes up too much time. 

I’ve had to become my own manager. If the system doesn't naturally offer the support a single mother needs, I’ve had to force the conversation. It’s draining, honestly. You’re already exhausted from the pregnancy, and then you have to perform this role of the "well-informed, calm, low-risk patient" just to make sure you aren't overlooked. It’s an exhausting act to keep up every single time I walk through those doors.
Were those barriers primarily practical, emotional, relational, or something else — and can you give me a concrete example?

It’s mostly a mix of the emotional and the structural, if that makes sense. The "transactional" nature of the appointments is a concrete barrier because it makes me feel like I can’t be honest about my mental state.

For example, last visit, I was feeling particularly overwhelmed because I’m struggling to find extra shifts at work to save money, and the financial pressure is just sitting on my chest like a lead weight. I wanted to say, "I’m terrified about the costs of a newborn and I feel like I'm drowning." But the midwife was already moving toward the door, checking her watch, and saying, "Everything looks great, no concerns on the charts." 

I physically couldn't bring myself to stop her and say, "Wait, I’m not okay." I just nodded and said, "Everything is fine." The barrier is that the system is designed for a body, not a person. It’s built to monitor a heartbeat and a blood pressure, but there’s no place for a single mother to voice the crushing weight of doing this alone. I’m navigating it by keeping a notebook, but it feels like I’m constantly fighting to be seen as more than just a successful pregnancy outcome. I’m a person who is actually struggling, and the system just isn't built to catch that.

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

My biggest goal was—and still is—to feel seen. I wanted my care team to look past the "low-risk" status on my chart and understand that the lack of risk in my vitals doesn't mean there's a lack of risk in my life. I wanted a space where I could say, "I’m scared of the nights alone," and have someone meet that with, "I hear you, and here is how we can support you." I wanted a partnership, not just a series of diagnostic check-ins.

To be honest, those goals haven't been met. The care is efficient, but it’s clinical to a fault. They’re doing a great job keeping me and the baby healthy, and I’m thankful for that, but I leave those appointments feeling just as isolated as when I walked in. I find myself doing the emotional labor for the providers—I hold back my anxieties so I don’t seem like a burden or an "anxious patient," which just makes me feel more alone in the process.

I’m still hoping for a shift, maybe as I get closer to the third trimester. I need them to ask, "How are *you* doing, Dian?" and actually wait for the answer. I don't need a therapist, but I do need my medical team to recognize that my health isn't just about my blood pressure; it's about my ability to survive this transition.
Can you describe a specific appointment or interaction that felt closest to what you had hoped care would look like?

There was one time, a few weeks back, when my regular midwife was running late and I had to see a different one—I think her name was Sarah. Usually, the appointments are so sterile, but she sat down on the stool and just... stayed there. She didn't have her hand on the door handle yet. 

She was looking at my chart and she saw that I had put "Never Married" and she didn't just breeze past it. She looked up and said, "Dian, you’re doing this all on your own. How are you managing the pressure of that?" 

It caught me so off guard I almost cried. It wasn't that she had a magic solution for the bills or the long nights, but just the fact that she acknowledged it—that she *saw* the weight I was carrying—made the room feel smaller, more intimate. We talked for five minutes about how I was handling the physical symptoms versus the mental ones. It didn't take any longer than a normal appointment, but it felt like a lifetime of difference. 

That was the moment I realized that "good care" isn't about more time; it’s about a human connection. She validated that my feelings were just as real as the baby’s heartbeat. I left that office feeling a little less like a chart number and a little more like a mother who had someone in her corner. That’s all I want.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

It’s like walking through a minefield. There is so much noise out there—mom-blogs, forums, apps with conflicting advice—and when you’re on your own, the noise is terrifying because you don’t have a second opinion to bounce things off of. 

I’ve had to develop a pretty strict "filter." If I’m looking for medical guidance, I stick to the big, reputable sources like the ACOG or the Mayo Clinic. I refuse to go down those rabbit holes on social media where everyone is sharing their "horror stories" or unsolicited warnings, because that just feeds my anxiety. I also look for consistency. If three different credible medical sources are saying the same thing about the Tdap booster, I trust it. 

When it comes to the "how-to" of motherhood, I’m much more guarded. I don’t trust just anyone’s opinion. I prioritize people I know who are actually living a similar life, even if I don't know many. But honestly, most of my trust is centered on my midwife—at least the ones who take that extra minute to acknowledge me as a person. If Sarah tells me something, I take it as gospel. I think I’ve realized that I trust information that acknowledges both the science *and* the reality of being a solo parent. If a source ignores the "human" element, I tend to be skeptical of it.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

The highs are like nothing else—that heartbeat on the monitor is a high that sustains me for days. But the anxieties? Those are constant, background noise. The uncertainty is the worst part. I’m constantly wondering, *Am I buying the right things? Am I going to recognize when something is wrong? Will I be enough?*

My care environment… it really hasn’t been built for this landscape. It’s built for the physical, not the emotional. It’s like they’re tracking my pregnancy in a vacuum where I’m just a biological system. When I’m in the exam room, I feel like I have to "perform" wellness—I smile, I say I’m fine, I hold the panic in—because the system feels so rushed. I’m managing my emotions almost entirely by myself, which is exhausting. I feel like I’m in a constant state of "masking" my fear so I don’t disrupt the clinical flow.

I wish there was a way to weave that emotional check-in into the routine. If someone asked me about my anxieties with the same casual tone they use to ask about my morning sickness, it would be a total game-changer. Right now, I’m doing the heavy lifting, and I’m just hoping I don’t buckle before the finish line.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

That would have to be around week eight, when the morning sickness hit me so hard I couldn’t even keep water down, and I had to miss two shifts at work. I was sitting on my kitchen floor in the dark, shivering and terrified that I was doing something wrong—that I wasn't eating enough, that I was failing this baby before it even really started. I called the office, and a nurse told me over the phone, "That’s normal, just keep hydrated and come in for your scheduled appointment in two weeks."

Logically, I knew she was right. But emotionally, I felt completely abandoned. I didn't need a medical intervention; I needed to know that someone understood how scared I was to be doing this while physically feeling like I was falling apart. I didn't need a clinical "normal," I needed a human "I’m here."

What would have helped? A simple follow-up, or even a nurse saying, "I know this is incredibly hard, especially on your own—is there anything you need right now?" It wouldn’t have changed the nausea, but it would have changed the isolation. It would have made me feel like I wasn't just a symptom to be managed, but a person navigating a really tough moment. Instead, I just felt like a burden.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

Every single decision feels like a high-stakes exam I’m taking by myself. Because I don't have a partner to talk through the pros and cons with, I feel like I have to know everything. For screenings and tests, I’ve relied heavily on the clinical guidelines—I want to know *everything* I can possibly know about the baby’s health, because information feels like safety. If the medical literature says a test is standard, I’m doing it, no questions asked. I’m almost afraid to say no to anything, as if saying "no" would be a sign that I’m not taking this seriously or that I’m being a "bad" mom.

The biggest influence, outside of the official clinical handbooks, has been that internal, desperate need for control. When I was deciding about certain genetic screenings, I read everything I could find. But the real decision-maker was the voice in my head saying, "Dian, you can't afford a surprise that you aren't prepared for." 

I really wish there was someone—a counselor or even a midwife with time—to sit with me and say, "You don't have to choose the most intense monitoring just because you’re scared." I’m making these calls based on anxiety rather than what’s actually best for my mental peace, and I don't think my care team even realizes that’s how I’m operating.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It’s really strange to watch your body shift when you’re doing it solo. My clothes are starting to feel tight, and every time I look in the mirror, I’m caught between being completely amazed and totally panicked. I’m proud of my body for doing this, for carrying that heartbeat, but I’m also mourning the version of me that was just... me. Now, I feel like I’m a vessel for someone else, and there's a vulnerability there that I wasn't prepared for. 

Within the care environment? No, those feelings are never addressed. To my midwives, these changes are just data. "Weight gain is on track," "Abdominal measurement is normal." It’s entirely physical. They don’t see that for me, these changes are deeply emotional reminders that my life is being fundamentally rewritten, and I’m the only one here to witness it. 

I’ve never had a provider ask me how I’m feeling about my changing body, or if I’m struggling with the image of myself as a mother-to-be. It’s always "You look great, see you next month." I know they mean well, but it’s just another example of how the clinical setting feels like a place where my heart and my head aren't invited—only my physical measurements. I feel like I’m just a shell being filled up, and nobody sees the person inside trying to keep her balance.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

It wasn’t the sickness or the fatigue that caught me off guard—those are in every book I’ve read. It was the feeling of being entirely "other" in my own skin. I feel like a guest in my own body, and I wasn't prepared for how much I would miss just being Dian, just being *me*. There's this intense, quiet pressure that because I’m doing this alone, I have to be the perfect vessel. I feel like my body isn't mine anymore; it belongs to the baby, to the doctors, to the "process."

The hardest part is the way I’ve started looking at my own reflection. I look in the mirror and I see my waist thickening and my clothes not fitting, and instead of feeling "glowing" or whatever the magazines say, I just feel exposed. I’m incredibly lonely in this transformation. I don't have a partner to tell me I’m beautiful or to help me navigate the weird, heavy feeling of my legs or the way I’m winded just walking up the stairs to my apartment. 

Nobody told me that your body can feel like a stranger, and it’s even harder when you have no one to share that strangeness with. In the clinic, they see the metrics, but they don’t see the way I hold my breath when I look at my stomach. I’m just a patient, and I think I’ve forgotten how to just be a person who’s having a hard time with the change.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

It’s a strange divide, honestly. My friends are lovely, but they’re living their own lives—jobs, relationships, the typical stuff—so when I try to talk about the deeper, sharper anxieties, I can see them sort of glaze over. They offer platitudes like "you’ll be a great mom," which is sweet, but it doesn't help me figure out the practical, terrifying "how-to" of being a solo parent. I’ve ended up leaning on them for the distractions, but I can't lean on them for the weight of my fears.

That’s why the medical team feels so vital yet so frustrating. My healthcare professionals are the only ones who have the "technical" answers to my life-or-death worries, but they lack the human warmth. My friends have the warmth, but they don’t have the technical expertise. 

It’s like I have two separate islands. One island gives me facts but treats me like a specimen, and the other island gives me love but doesn't quite "get" the gravity of the medical reality I'm navigating. I feel like I’m constantly commuting between them, trying to bridge the gap myself. What I really need is for the medical team to just *be* that bridge—to offer the clinical expertise *with* the kind of compassion that says, "I understand why you're scared, and you aren't doing this alone." I’m still waiting for someone to actually hold both for me.
Can you describe a moment when informal support made a meaningful difference to your experience — what happened and why did it matter?

A few weeks ago, I was in one of those really dark, suffocating spirals. I had just come back from a check-up where I felt like the midwife was barely listening, and I was sitting on my bed staring at my laundry, just paralyzed by the thought that I didn't know how I was going to manage everything once the baby arrives. I ended up texting a friend of mine, not even sure what I was looking for, just needing to let the fear out so I wouldn't choke on it.

She didn't try to fix it, and she didn't give me medical advice I didn't ask for. She just told me, "Dian, you are allowed to be terrified. You’re doing the biggest, hardest thing a person can do, and you’re doing it by yourself. It’s okay to be overwhelmed." 

That simple acknowledgment meant the world to me. It wasn't about her helping me pack a diaper bag or drive me to an appointment; it was about her validating that my struggle was real and that I wasn't failing because I was afraid. It took the edge off the panic. It made me realize that even if I am technically "alone," I’m not completely isolated. That tiny bit of human recognition gave me the strength to get up off the bed and just... breathe. It was a reminder that I’m still a person, not just a set of vitals.

I think about the future a lot. People keep telling me, "Once the baby is here, everything will fall into place," but that honestly terrifies me. What if it doesn’t? What if the reality of those long, silent nights in my apartment is exactly as heavy as I’m imagining? 

The care I’ve received so far has been so focused on the *now*—the vitamins, the scans, the blood pressure—that I feel like I’m being marched toward a cliff without a parachute. I’m doing the work to be a "good patient," but I’m exhausted by it. I find myself wondering if there's ever going to be a moment where the focus shifts from "Is the baby healthy?" to "Is the mother okay?" 

I’m starting to realize that my anxiety isn’t just a personality trait; it’s a symptom of navigating this alone. I wish the healthcare system saw that. I wish they understood that when a single woman comes in with a list of questions, she’s not just "high-strung"—she’s trying to outsource the support she doesn't have at home. 

I’m hopeful, don’t get me wrong. Every time I think about that flickering heartbeat, I know I’ll figure this out. I’ve always been independent, and I’ll be independent for this baby, too. But I really, really hope that as I move further along, I find at least one person in that clinic who sees me for who I am: a scared, excited, determined woman who is just trying to do the best she can with what she’s got. That, more than any vitamin or scan, is what would make me feel like I’m actually going to be okay.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

There wasn't a conversation. That’s the thing people usually assume, isn't it? They think there’s a table with two people sitting across from each other, weighing the pros and cons, deciding if they're "ready." For me, it wasn't a discussion—it was an unplanned reality that hit me like a lightning strike. 

When I saw that positive test, the "idea" of having a baby shifted from a distant, vague "maybe someday" to an immediate, heartbeat-in-my-body urgency. There was no one to ask, "Are we ready?" or "How will we handle the mortgage?" It was just me, the bathroom light humming overhead, and this terrifying, exhilarating thought: *This is happening. Right now.*

The "conversations" I had were entirely internal. I spent those first few days pacing my apartment, talking to the walls, trying to bargain with the universe. I went through every single fear—the money, the isolation, the fact that I’d be the one waking up at 3:00 AM alone. It was lonely, honestly. There was no one to share the initial shock or the initial joy. I had to build my own foundation in those first few days, just accepting that my life was changing course. It was like I was holding a secret that was both the most precious thing I’d ever known and the scariest thing I’d ever faced.

I’ve realized that since I don’t have a partner to share this with, the medical team is essentially the only mirror I have. That sounds strange, I know, but when you’re doing this alone, you rely on them to confirm that you’re doing a "good job." Every clean bill of health I get at an appointment feels like a gold star—a sign that despite my messy, solitary reality, I’m succeeding as a mother.

It’s almost like I’m performing for them. I walk in with my lists, I ask the right questions about Tdap and flu shots, I stay calm and professional—all to signal, *“Look, I’m a capable woman, don't worry about me.”* But there’s a part of me that’s desperately craving for one of them to stop the performance. I want them to look at the "Never Married" box on my chart, look at my face, and say, “I know this is a lot for one person to carry, and we’re here for you.” 

If they did that, I think I could finally drop the armor. I wouldn't have to be the “perfect, low-risk patient” anymore. I could just be Dian, a woman who is doing her best to build a future in an empty apartment. I really hope that, as I move into the second trimester, I find that connection. Because right now, the silence between the medical facts is where all the real, heavy stuff is hiding.
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

I’m proud of my resilience. Even on the days when the nausea is so bad I feel like I’m fading away, I still get up, I still go to work, and I still show up for my prenatal appointments. Every time I hear that heartbeat, I think, *“My body is doing this. It’s creating life.”* That feels like a quiet kind of superpower, especially when I’m the only one in the room to witness it.

But the anxiety? That’s mostly about control. I’m constantly worried that my body is going to "betray" me—that I’ll develop a complication, or that I’ll get sick, or that I’ll be too weak to handle everything on my own. Because I don't have a partner to watch over me, I feel like I have to be the one who senses every tiny shift and reports it to the midwife immediately. If I feel a weird pain, I’m not just worried about the pain—I’m terrified it means I’m failing the baby.

And there’s this deep uncertainty about how my body is going to change, not just now, but after. I look at my stomach sometimes and I feel like it’s a map to a place I’ve never been. I don't know who I’ll be once this is all over. Will I still be me? Will I be the same woman who walks down South Main Street with her head held high, or will I be someone entirely consumed by the exhaustion of motherhood? It’s a lot to hold, and I’m doing it all without a witness.

You know, the more I talk about this, the more I realize that the silence is really the hardest part. It’s like being in a room where everyone is speaking a language you don’t quite know yet—the language of pregnancy, of motherhood—and I’m trying to learn it while simultaneously being tested on it every single day. 

I’m starting to think that what I’m really seeking from my care team isn’t just medical expertise. It’s a sort of companionship. I want them to acknowledge the sheer *weight* of this transition. When I walk into that clinic, I’m carrying more than just a developing baby; I’m carrying the anxiety of a future I have to map out alone. If they could just pause for a second—not to look at the blood pressure cuff, but to look at me—and validate that this is a heavy road to walk solo, I think I’d feel so much lighter. 

I’m trying to be strong, and I think I am—I’m getting my vaccinations, I’m reading the guidelines, I’m showing up. But I’m also just a woman who needs to know that when the really hard days come, I won't have to navigate them in total isolation. I really hope that as my belly grows, the way I’m seen in that office changes, too. I want to be a patient who is heard, not just a chart that is read.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

I remember a check-up a few weeks back. I had compiled a list of questions about the Tdap booster and some concerns I had regarding my energy levels at work. When I pulled out my notebook to read them, the provider—a doctor I hadn't met before—actually chuckled. It wasn’t a mean laugh, exactly, but it was dismissive. He said, "You first-time moms are all the same, Dian. You read too much on the internet and you think you need to control everything. Just relax and trust the process."

I felt like I had been slapped. He didn't see that my "research" wasn't about being a neurotic first-timer; it was about being a woman who doesn't have a partner to help her navigate the unknown. I was doing that work to keep my head above water, and he made me feel like an annoying, hysterical nuisance. 

I couldn't say anything back. I just folded up my notebook, nodded, and listened to his lecture about "not worrying so much." I left that room feeling smaller than when I walked in. It confirmed my worst fear: that because I'm on my own, my concerns are viewed as "anxiety" rather than valid, necessary questions. It was condescending, and it stripped me of the little bit of agency I was fighting so hard to hold onto.

I think that incident changed how I approach my appointments now. I’m way more guarded. After he dismissed me like that, I started asking myself if I should even bother bringing up the things that truly bother me. It’s safer to just ask the “correct” medical questions and keep the rest of the noise locked up inside. I feel like I have to filter my own personality just to be respected, which is exhausting when you’re already putting everything you have into just showing up.

It makes me wonder if there’s a whole group of women out there—especially single ones—who are just going through the motions, hiding their biggest fears because they’ve been told to “just relax.” Being told to relax when you’re facing the biggest transition of your life without a partner isn’t advice; it’s an erasure of my reality. 

I’m still going to show up to every check-up, and I’m still going to be the “perfect patient” on paper because I have to be. But my heart isn't in those interactions anymore. I’m just waiting for the day when I can finally find someone who understands that my preparation isn't a symptom of something wrong with me—it’s the only way I know how to make sure my baby and I come out on the other side of this whole. That's the dream, really—a doctor who respects my process instead of laughing at it.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mom and I have a complicated relationship, and we haven't always been close, especially regarding the life path I’m on now. When I told her, I didn’t get that warm, cinematic “congratulations.” I got a lot of worried silence and questions about how I’m going to manage, which honestly just made my own fears feel more real and more heavy. She comes from a generation where you did things in a very specific order—marriage, house, baby—and I think she’s struggling to see how this fits into that framework.

Because of that, I’ve had to tune out a lot of the “advice” I’ve received from that older generation. It’s mostly just critiques disguised as wisdom, like “you’ll be tired,” or “how are you going to afford this?” It’s not useful, and it definitely doesn't support the strength I’m trying to cultivate. 

I’ve had to distance myself from those stories because they carry too much judgment. I’m trying to write my own script now, and listening to their baggage just makes me feel like I’m already starting behind. I’ve realized that I have to be my own mentor. I read, I research, I listen to my body, and I try to ignore the voices that tell me I’m doing it the “wrong” way. I’m doing it *my* way, and that has to be enough.

You know, listening to myself talk about all this—the distance from my mom, the dismissive doctors, the solitary nights in Fall River—I realize I’ve spent my entire pregnancy trying to build a fortress. I think if I can just be informed enough, careful enough, and controlled enough, I can prove that my "unconventional" start doesn’t mean a "failed" outcome. 

But I’m also realizing that the fortress is getting really heavy. Every time I walk down Main Street feeling that salty air, I feel like I’m holding my breath, waiting for the "real" part to start—the part where I’m a mom and I don't have a partner to pass the baby to when I’m exhausted. 

I think the biggest thing I’ve learned—or maybe the thing I’m still trying to learn—is that I don’t have to do it *all* perfectly. I just need to be honest about where I am. I’m scared, I’m lonely, and I’m incredibly proud of myself. If I could just find a provider who truly understood that, I think I could exhale for the first time since I saw those two lines on the test. 

I’m hopeful that as I keep showing up, I’ll find that connection. I’m not just a set of vitals. I’m a woman who’s choosing to do this, and I’m going to make sure that my baby and I are okay, even if I have to navigate the path entirely on my own.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

I’ve found myself going back to the ACOG website and the Mayo Clinic, almost obsessively. It feels safe there. There’s no opinion, no "you should do it this way," and no judgment about my marital status. Just cold, hard medical facts. In my life right now, facts are the only things that don't shift when I move. 

When I’m feeling really lost, I sometimes visit those reputable pregnancy apps that track the baby’s development week by week. Seeing that my baby is the size of a prune or a lime—that physical transformation—makes it feel real and manageable. It bridges the gap between what I’m feeling inside and the clinical reality outside.

But I’ve completely pulled away from forums or social media groups. I once checked a Reddit thread for single moms, thinking it would be supportive, but all it did was amplify my worst fears—the stories of burnout, the financial strain, the exhaustion. I don't need more proof that this is hard; I already know it’s hard. I need tools to survive it. 

I’m very selective about what I let into my brain. If a source makes me feel panicked or insecure, I close it immediately. If it gives me actionable, evidence-based steps I can take to keep myself healthy, I bookmark it. I’m curate-ing my own reality because, quite frankly, I can’t afford the chaos. I’m the project manager of this pregnancy, and I’m very protective of the data.

I think what I’ve realized through all this is that my information-seeking is really just a way of trying to compensate for the lack of a village. When you don't have a partner to turn to at 10 PM and ask, "Is this cramp normal?", you have to turn to Google or ACOG. It’s not the same as having a human hand to hold, but it’s the closest thing I’ve got to a safety net.

I’m so protective of those "reputable" sources because they don’t judge me for being single. They don’t look at my marital status and wonder if I’m "ready." They just see a pregnancy, a set of dates, and a list of medical requirements. In a way, the internet is more compassionate than some of the people I’ve dealt with in person. It doesn’t have an opinion on my life choices; it just gives me the facts I need to get through the next week.

I do find myself wishing, though, that there was a bridge between that cold, hard data and the emotional reality. I want a source—or a person—who can say, "Yes, the Tdap booster is medically indicated, *and* I know it’s hard to do this by yourself, so here is a resource for postpartum support." That’s the missing piece. I have the medical handbook, and I have my own resolve, but I’m still waiting for that extra layer of human support that makes the journey feel a little less lonely. I’m doing okay, I think, but I’m ready for someone to finally see the full picture.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

It wasn't a single moment, but rather the cumulative way the clinic staff interacts with me during every intake. I’m always asked, "Is there a partner joining you today?" and when I say "No," the energy in the room shifts. Sometimes it’s pity, sometimes it’s just this rapid-fire, slightly patronizing tone, like they’re trying to make things "simpler" for me because they assume I’m overwhelmed.

I remember discussing genetic screening options. I had done my research—I knew the statistics, the false positive rates, everything—but the doctor kept framing his answers in a way that felt like he was talking to a teenager. He kept saying, "Don't worry your head about the technical side, Dian, just let us handle the logistics." 

It infuriated me. I *am* the logistics! I’m the one managing the rent, the work shifts, the nutrition, and the prenatal care alone. I didn't need him to "simplify" it; I needed him to give me the information so I could make the call. Being treated like I’m incapable of understanding the medical reality just because I’m doing this solo is such a heavy, hidden insult. It makes me feel like my competence as a person is being erased by my status as a single mother. I’m not a child—I’m a woman making the biggest decision of her life. I just wish the medical staff treated me with that same level of respect.

It’s really painful, actually, because I know they see that "Never Married" box and they make their own assumptions about my stability or my intelligence. They think, *“Here’s a young woman, she’s doing this solo, she’s probably overwhelmed, let’s make it easy for her.”* But they don't realize that in doing so, they’re stripping away the one thing that keeps me feeling powerful: my autonomy. 

If I’m not a capable decision-maker in their eyes, then my anxiety, my preparation, and my research feel like they’re being reduced to "emotional outbursts." It’s like they see my competence as a threat, or maybe just an inconvenience. I just want them to look me in the eye and treat me like the person who is running the show, because I *am*. 

I find myself walking out of those appointments feeling like I have to apologize for knowing too much, for asking too many questions, or for being too "involved." That shouldn't be how this works. I’m carrying a life, I’m managing my own future, and I deserve to be treated like an adult who is fully capable of understanding what’s happening in her own body. I shouldn't have to work this hard to be respected. I’m doing the heavy lifting, and all I ask is that they don’t try to take my brain away from me while they’re at it.
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

My phone is essentially my lifeline. I don’t have a partner to turn to, so that little screen is where I keep my lists, my calendar, my research, and my reminders. It’s private, it’s mine, and I guard it carefully. When I'm in my apartment in Fall River, feeling that weight of being on my own, I use it to look up everything from nutrition to the Tdap schedule. It feels like the only space where I can be completely honest about my fears without being judged, because I can search for "how to handle being a single mom" without having to look anyone in the eye and feel their pity or their assumptions.

It hasn't changed, and I don't think it will. If anything, I'm more glued to it now than I was before I was pregnant. It’s the one tool I have that I don’t have to justify to anyone. I don’t have to explain why I’m looking something up to a partner, and I don't have to worry about a medical provider hovering over my shoulder while I'm doing my own research. It’s the one place where I can be my own advocate, completely uninterrupted. It’s not a substitute for real human support, but it’s a constant, reliable presence in a life that feels like it’s constantly changing.

I suppose I’ve turned my phone into a surrogate partner of sorts. It doesn't have an opinion on my marital status, and it doesn't look at me with that "oh, you poor thing" expression when I mention I’m doing this on my own. It just holds the information I need to stay afloat. 

Sometimes, late at night, when the apartment feels incredibly quiet, I’ll just scroll through my notes—the ones where I’ve broken down my budget, the vaccine schedules, the questions for the next visit. Looking at that list makes me feel like I’m actually accomplishing something, that I’m not just drifting through this. It’s my way of exerting power over a situation that can feel really chaotic. 

The privacy is everything, too. I don't think I could handle someone looking over my shoulder, questioning why I’m so focused on the details. To them, it might look like I’m just "anxious," but to me, it’s about survival. It’s my way of making sure that, despite the lack of a village, I am building something sturdy for this baby. My phone is the one place where I’m not "Dian the single, low-risk, first-time mom"—I’m just Dian, the person who is going to make sure everything gets done. And honestly, for now, that’s enough.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

I’ll never forget when I had these sharp, stabbing pains in my lower abdomen around my twelfth week. I’d read that some cramping is normal, but this felt different—more intense. I went to the clinic, my heart racing, and the provider barely looked up from his chart while he palpated my stomach for all of ten seconds. He told me it was just "round ligament pain" and to "go home and put your feet up." 

He didn't explain *why* it wasn't something else; he just dismissed it as a standard annoyance of pregnancy. I felt so small in that moment, and so incredibly guilty for even asking. It was like he was implying that I was just being dramatic or that I didn't know the difference between "normal" and "concerning." 

It made me feel like I couldn't trust my own body. If a professional tells me my pain is nothing, I start to second-guess myself, even when my gut is screaming that something feels off. That dismissal left me feeling isolated and terrified—not just because of the pain, but because I realized I couldn't rely on my care team to be my safety net. It made me feel like if I were to actually have a complication, they wouldn't believe me until it was too late. I walked out of that office feeling like I had to be my own doctor, and that’s a heavy, lonely burden to carry.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for asking. I think what’s most important to leave you with is that my anxiety isn’t a flaw in my personality—it’s a byproduct of my reality. When you are doing this alone, every single decision feels like you’re trying to prevent a catastrophe. I’m not asking for someone to take over for me; I’m just asking for a partner in this process who sees me as a whole person, not just a set of clinical charts.

I want the system to understand that being "low-risk" physically doesn't mean being "low-need" emotionally. If I walk into an appointment and I seem "too" informed or "too" prepared, don't laugh at me or tell me to just relax. Ask me why I'm doing that work. Ask me how I’m managing the weight of the silence at home. 

Being a single mother by choice—or even just by circumstance—is a strength. I’m building a life from scratch, and I’m doing it well. I just need my healthcare team to see that strength and respect it, rather than trying to diminish it by making me feel like an "anxious, neurotic first-timer." We’re a team, or at least we should be. I’m carrying the baby, but I’m also carrying the responsibility of being both parents. A little bit of empathy goes a long way, and I really hope that for the women coming after me, the clinical experience feels a little more human.
